cues.x: Welcome.
Today we are going deep on a small book that has quietly saved more startups from wasting years of their lives than almost anything else on the shelf.
It is called The Mom Test, How to Talk to Customers and Learn if Your Business is a Good Idea When Everyone is Lying to You, and it was written by Rob Fitzpatrick.

If you are building something, a product, an app, a service, a side project you hope will one day pay your rent, this is the book that teaches you how to have the conversations that actually tell you the truth.
And here is the uncomfortable thing you are going to sit with for the next hour.
Almost every conversation you have had so far about your idea has been lying to you.
Not because people are cruel.
Because people are kind.
And kindness, it turns out, is poison for a founder who is trying to figure out what is real.
Let me start with the story that gives the book its name, because it captures everything.
Imagine you have an idea for an app.
It is a cookbook app for the iPad.
The pitch is that your mom loves to cook, she has all these recipes, and wouldn't it be wonderful if she could keep them organized on this beautiful glowing screen in the kitchen.
So you go to the person who loves you most in the entire world.
You go to your mom.
And you say, Mom, I have an idea for an app.
It is a cookbook for your iPad.
Do you think that is a good idea?
And what does your mom say?
She says, oh honey, that sounds wonderful.
Of course she does.
She loves you.
She would rather cut off her own arm than tell you that your baby is ugly.
She is going to say it sounds great, she would definitely use it, and she is so proud of you.
And you walk away from that kitchen glowing, feeling validated, feeling like you have data.
You do not have data.
You have a mother.
That is the whole problem in a single scene.
The people around you are emotionally invested in not hurting your feelings, and so they tell you what you want to hear.
And the tragic part is that it is not just your mom.
It is your friends, your coworkers, the nice person you cornered at a networking event, the investor being polite, even strangers who just want the awkward conversation to end.
Everyone is, in their own gentle way, lying to you.
So the question Fitzpatrick asks is brilliant.
He does not say, how do we get people to stop lying.
You cannot control other people.
Instead he flips it.
He says, how do we ask questions in such a way that even our mom cannot lie to us.
Questions so well constructed that they are immune to the bias of the person answering.
That is The Mom Test.
A set of rules for talking to people about your idea that are good even when you are talking to your own mother, because the questions themselves do not give her the room to flatter you.
It was never her job to tell you the truth.
It was always your job to ask well enough that she could not help but hand it to you anyway.
So let me give you the three rules right up front, and then we are going to spend real time inside each one, because the whole book lives in these three.
Rule one.
Talk about their life instead of your idea.
Rule two.
Ask about specifics in the past instead of generics or opinions about the future.
And rule three.
Talk less and listen more.
That is it.
Those three rules are the spine.

If you remembered nothing else from this entire episode, remembered those three, and actually practiced them, you would be ahead of ninety percent of the founders out there who are still walking into rooms, pitching, and misreading polite nods as market validation.
Let me sit on rule one for a moment.
Talk about their life instead of your idea.
The instinct, the deep gravitational pull every founder feels, is to talk about the idea.
You love the idea.
You have been thinking about it in the shower.
You want to describe it, defend it, get people excited about it.
But the moment you describe your idea, you have poisoned the well.
Because now the person is not reacting to reality.
They are reacting to you, to your enthusiasm, to the social pressure of the moment.
And they will be nice.
So the trick is to keep the conversation on their life, their problems, their day, the way they currently do things.
You are a detective, not a salesperson.
Your job in these early conversations is not to sell the idea.
It is not even to mention the idea if you can avoid it.
Your job is to learn whether the problem you imagine they have is actually a problem they have, whether it hurts enough that they have done something about it, and how they live around it right now.
Let me replay that kitchen conversation the right way, because the contrast is everything.
This time the son never mentions his app at all.
He just gets curious about his mom.
He says, Mom, how do you cook these days, walk me through the last meal you made from a brand new recipe.
And she admits, honestly, that she does not, really.
She meant to try that recipe from the weekend paper, but it looked fiddly, so she made the same pasta she always makes.
And he asks, what do you actually use the iPad for.

Well, it mostly sits on the counter, she reads the news on it, and the last time she tried to follow a recipe on it the screen went dark halfway through while her hands were covered in flour, so she gave up and pulled out her old splattered cookbook instead.
Now that is gold.
In two minutes of asking about her real life, he learned more than a hundred sounds lovelys could ever tell him.
He learned she rarely cooks new things, that the screen going dark is a genuine little rage, that her paper cookbook already wins.
None of it is flattering.
All of it is true.
And he got it precisely by refusing to talk about his own precious idea.
Here is why this matters so much.
If you talk about your idea, the best you can get is an opinion.
And opinions are worthless.
I am going to say that again because it is central.
Opinions are worthless.
When someone says, I think that is a great idea, or I would definitely use that, they are giving you a compliment or a prediction, and neither of those is data you can build a company on.
What you want instead are facts about their life.
What they actually did last Tuesday.
How much they currently spend solving this problem.
What tools they have already downloaded, already tried, already abandoned.
Facts about the past are real.
Opinions about the future are fiction dressed up as helpfulness.
Now let me pull rule two apart, because it is the one people get wrong most often.
Ask about specifics in the past instead of generics or opinions about the future.
This follows straight from the first rule.
The single deadliest question in all of business is, would you buy this.
Because the honest answer is, nobody actually knows.
We are all wildly, hopelessly optimistic about our future selves.
Ask someone, would you use an app that helps you exercise, and of course they say yes, future me is disciplined, future me goes to the gym.
Ask instead, when did you last go to the gym, and what happened, and you find out present me has not been in four months.
Anything in the future tense is a wish.
Only the past is evidence.
Watch the difference in the cookbook case too.
Bad question, do you ever have trouble keeping your recipes organized.
That invites a generic, do you ever, and people will construct a flattering generalization.
Sure, yeah, sometimes, I guess.
Good question, talk me through the last time you tried to cook something from a recipe you had saved.
Now they cannot generalize.
They have to remember.
And when they remember, they tell you the truth, because the past is concrete.
They tell you, oh, well actually I just have a shoebox of printed recipes and I never look at them, or actually I just google it every time and I never save anything.
And there, in that answer, your beautiful cookbook app just died, or just got a lot more interesting, but either way you learned something real.
It helps to hear this in a different business, so let me move out of the kitchen.
Say you are building software to do the books for small shops.
The lazy question is, do you think doing your books is a hassle.
Everyone says yes, because a hassle is free to agree with.
The Mom Test version is, walk me through how you handled your books last month.

And suddenly you find out they do not do them monthly at all, they cram receipts in a shoebox and panic every spring, they already pay a bookkeeper eighty dollars a month who they secretly resent, or, just as usefully, that it takes them ten painless minutes in a spreadsheet and they feel no pain whatsoever.
One question buys you a polite yes.
The other buys you their actual life, their actual workaround, and the actual dollars already moving.
Only one of those tells you whether there is a business here.
Fitzpatrick hammers this point.
Anytime someone tells you what they always do, or what they usually do, or what they would do, be suspicious.
Generic claims are lies waiting to happen.
I always read the ingredients before I buy.
I would definitely pay for that.
I usually plan my meals for the week.
These are not facts.
These are self image.
People describe the person they wish they were, not the person they are.
The word would is the biggest red flag in the entire language of customer conversations.
I would buy that.
I would use that.
I would recommend it to everyone.
Every time you hear would, a little alarm should go off, because would is a hypothetical, and hypotheticals are free.
It costs a person nothing to say they would do something in an imaginary future.
So you gently, quietly, steer them back into the past.
Instead of, would you buy this, you ask, how are you dealing with this right now.
Instead of, do you think this is a problem, you ask, tell me about the last time this happened.
What did you do.
How much did it cost you, in time or money or frustration.
Have you looked for a way to fix it.
Have you paid for anything that tried to fix it.
That last one is gold, and I want to underline it.
Have you already tried to solve this, and did you spend money on it.
Because if someone has a real, painful problem, they have almost always already jury rigged some solution.
They have a spreadsheet held together with tape.
They pay for a tool they hate.
They have googled it a hundred times.
Evidence of past effort and past spending is the single most reliable signal that a problem is real.
If someone has a problem so mild that they have never once done anything about it, then they are not going to buy your solution to it either, no matter how sweetly they nod at your pitch.
On the flip side, if someone is currently paying fifty dollars a month for a clunky, ugly tool that half solves their problem, that is a person whose wallet is already open.
That is a fact.
Facts, not feelings.
Rule three, and it is the hardest, because it is about you.
Talk less, listen more.
You walked into that conversation wanting one thing, a yes.
Fitzpatrick calls this the quest for approval, and it is the poison in the whole well.

When you are secretly hunting for a yes, you pitch instead of ask, you talk over every pause, and the instant someone tosses you a lukewarm compliment, you grab it and run before they can complicate it with the truth.
But you are not there to be validated.
You are there to learn.
In a good conversation the other person is talking most of the time while you shut up and take notes.
New founders talk way too much.
They fill silences.
They explain.
They lead the witness.

The discipline is to ask a good question and then actually be quiet and let the other person fill the space, even when it gets a little uncomfortable, because the gold often comes right after the pause, when they think of the real example and start telling you the messy truth of how they actually live.
Here is the tell.
If you leave the meeting feeling fantastic, get suspicious.
If you leave with a page full of uncomfortable facts about someone's real life, that was a great meeting.
Now let me sharpen the facts versus feelings line even more, because the book keeps returning to it, and it names three specific traps that ambush these conversations.
Once you can hear them, you genuinely cannot un hear them.
These are the three types of bad data.
Compliments, fluff, and ideas.

I want you to be able to recognize each one the instant it appears in a conversation, the way a sommelier recognizes a wine, because the moment you recognize bad data, you can steer back to the good stuff.
The first type of bad data is compliments.
That is a great idea.
I love that.
You are so smart.
That is really cool.
You are really onto something.
Compliments feel like progress, and they are nothing.
They are the sound a conversation makes when somebody is being polite on their way out the door.
When a founder walks out of a meeting glowing and says, that went really well, they usually mean, I got a lot of compliments.
And compliments are worthless because, as we said, people are nice, and because a compliment is aimed at you and your ego, not at the problem and the world.
Fitzpatrick describes founders getting high on fake praise, coming back from a demo buzzing because everyone said it was cool, and then confusing that warm feeling for progress.
The dangerous thing about compliments is that they are the reward we are secretly fishing for.
We want to be told we are clever.
So we ask leading questions, we pitch, we perform, and we harvest a pile of compliments, and we go home feeling like a genius, having learned absolutely nothing.
The rule here is simple and a little painful.
Compliments are worthless.
When you get one, deflect it and dig.
Someone says, wow, cool idea.
You say, thanks, but honestly I am trying to figure out if it is even useful.
Can you tell me about the last time you ran into this problem yourself.
You do not bank the compliment.
You spend it, immediately, on getting back to their real life.
The second type of bad data is fluff, and fluff has fingerprints.
Listen for the words I would, I always, and I never.
I would totally buy that.
I always shop local.
I would never pay for something like this.
These are generic, hypothetical, aspirational statements about an imaginary version of a person, and they are almost always false, or at the very least completely unmeasured.
Fluff feels like information but it is smoke.
When someone drifts into fluff, they have left the world of facts and entered the world of imagination, and their imagination is optimistic and eager to please.
The move when you hear fluff is to anchor them back to a specific moment in the past.
They say, oh I would totally use an app like that.
You say, when was the last time you were in a situation where that would have helped, walk me through it.
And suddenly the fluffy cloud has to condense into a real raindrop, a real Tuesday, a real thing that happened, and you watch a confident I always dissolve into, well, okay, this one time.
The third type of bad data is ideas.
Now this one surprises people, because ideas seem good.
You are talking to a potential customer and they get excited and start flinging feature requests at you.
Oh, you should add this, you should integrate with that, you should have a social feature, you should let me export to PDF.
And it feels wonderful, because they are engaged, they are creative, they are on your team, and it is so tempting to scribble them all down as a to do list.
Do not.
Here is the trap.
You are the entrepreneur, not them.
A flood of feature ideas from a customer is not a roadmap.
It is a signal that they are engaged with a problem, which is useful, but the ideas themselves are mostly noise, and if you go home and build everything they asked for, you will build a Frankenstein.
The idea itself is usually worthless.
What is valuable is the problem or the emotion buried underneath it.
When someone demands a feature, dig for the why.
What would that let you do.
When did not having it last bite you.
The request is just smoke.
Go find the fire.
You capture the motivation, not the request, because the request is just their guess at a solution, and guessing at solutions is your job, informed by their problems.
So there is your radar.
Compliments, fluff, and ideas.
The three types of bad data.
And the skill, the actual skill you build over dozens of conversations, is hearing one of these in real time and gently, without being weird about it, steering the conversation back to real data.
Back to the past.
Back to facts.
Back to what they actually did and actually spent and actually felt.
Now let me talk about the two great dangers, the two ways a founder blows up a perfectly good conversation.
Fitzpatrick calls them the trap of pitching and the product landmine.
The trap of pitching is the one you already understand in your gut.
You go in meaning to learn, but you love your idea so much that at the first opening you start selling.
You start describing the vision.
Your eyes light up.
And the moment you do that, the person across from you shifts from being an honest witness to being a polite audience.
They stop telling you about their life and start reacting to your dream, and reactions to dreams are always kind and always useless.
The discipline is to keep your idea in your pocket for as long as humanly possible.
The longer you can go without revealing what you are building, the more honest and useful the conversation stays.
This is genuinely hard, because every fiber of you wants to share the thing you are proud of.
But you have to think of your idea like a secret you are protecting, not because it might get stolen, but because the moment you reveal it, you contaminate the very data you came to collect.
The product landmine is subtler and just as deadly.
It is when the conversation drifts toward your specific solution too early, and you start getting feedback on the product instead of learning about the problem.

You show a mockup, and now everyone is talking about the button color and whether the menu should be on the left, and you feel productive, but you have skipped the only question that matters, which is whether anyone has this problem badly enough to care about any solution at all.
You can polish a product no one needs into a jewel, and it will still be a product no one needs.

So you keep the conversation on the problem, on their world, on their behavior, and you resist the seductive pull of talking about your beautiful thing until you have earned the right by understanding that the problem is real.
There is a small, perfect example the book uses to show how false positives sneak in, and it is one you will recognize from your own life.
Someone says, we should grab lunch sometime.
It feels like a connection.
It feels like the meeting went great.
But we should grab lunch sometime is the classic polite brush off.
It is a compliment wrapped in a fake future commitment, and it means nothing, because nothing was actually committed.
Compare that to someone who pulls out their calendar and says, how about Thursday at noon, here is my number, text me.
One of those is real, and one of those is fluff, and a naive founder counts them the same and walks away thinking they have ten interested customers when they actually have zero.
This brings us to maybe the most practically important idea in the whole book, and it separates people who are genuinely testing a business from people who are just collecting nice feelings.
Here is the concept.
Talk is cheap.
A conversation, however warm, proves absolutely nothing until the other person gives up something they actually value.
Fitzpatrick calls this commitment and advancement.
A commitment is when a customer proves they are serious by paying you in one of three currencies.
Time, reputation, or money.
Advancement is when the relationship moves to a real next step, instead of dissolving into a vague, let us stay in touch.
The currency of real interest is time, reputation, and money, because those are the three things people do not give away for free.
Let me break the three currencies down.
Time is agreeing to a real follow up with a clear purpose, or sitting down to give you serious feedback, or trialing your thing for two weeks.
Reputation is introducing you to their boss, their team, five colleagues, putting their own name on the line by vouching for you.
And money is a pre order, a deposit, a letter of intent, actual dollars on the table.
The beautiful thing about all three is that they cost something, and because they cost something, people simply will not spend them just to be polite.
Your mom will call your idea lovely all day long.
She is far, far less likely to hand you fifty dollars, or introduce you to ten of her friends, or block out next Tuesday afternoon for you.
A compliment is what people give you when they want you to leave happy.
A commitment is what they give you when they actually care.
This reframes the entire purpose of a meeting.
A meeting is not a success because it was pleasant.
A meeting is a success if it either advances toward a real commitment or gives you a clear, useful no.

A pleasant meeting that ends with no commitment and no clear next step is actually a failure dressed up as a win, and those are the most dangerous meetings of all, because they feel good and teach you nothing and let you keep fooling yourself.
So the real question at the end of a good conversation is never, did they like it.
It is, what did they give up to prove it.
And the magic little phrase to keep in your back pocket is, so what happens next.
You say it, and then you shut up, and their answer tells you everything.
If they scramble to set up the next thing, you have something.
If they get vague and mumble, you have your answer too, and it is a gift, because a fast, honest no saves you months.
Let me talk about how to actually structure these conversations, because the book is intensely practical about this.
Before you ever sit down with someone, Fitzpatrick says you should prepare your big three questions.
The three most important things you need to learn from this type of person that, if you do not learn them, could kill your business.
Not a script.
Not twenty questions.
The three scariest, most important unknowns.
And you prepare them in advance, because in the moment, in the warmth of a real conversation, you will forget them, you will get swept up, you will chase interesting tangents.
Having your big three written down and clear before you walk in keeps you honest and makes sure you leave with the answers that actually matter.
And these should be the questions you are afraid to ask, the ones where the answer might destroy your whole premise.
Those are exactly the ones you need most, because you are trying to find out if your business is a bad idea before you spend two years discovering it the expensive way.
And here is a liberating idea.
Keep it casual.
So many founders think they need a formal meeting, a scheduled thirty minutes, a whole production.
But formal meetings raise the stakes, make people nervous, and paradoxically make them more polite and less honest.
The best customer learning often happens in casual, low stakes moments.
A quick chat.
A coffee.
A conversation at an event where you are just curious about how they do their work.
When it is casual, there is no pressure to perform, no expectation that you are pitching, so people relax and tell you the truth about their lives.

Fitzpatrick's guidance is to avoid the formal meeting whenever you can, and to strip the whole thing down so it feels like a genuine chat between two humans, one of whom is deeply curious about the other's problems.
If it feels like a meeting, you have already made it harder to get truth.
If it feels like a conversation, truth flows.
Now let me connect this to one of the quieter but most powerful ideas in the book, which is about who exactly you are talking to.
This is customer segmentation, but Fitzpatrick makes it visceral.
A huge number of founders describe their customer as everyone.
My app is for everyone who cooks.
My tool is for all small businesses.

And everyone is a trap, because if your customer is everyone, then you cannot find them, you cannot talk to them, and you cannot figure out what they specifically need, because everyone needs different things.
So the work is to go from a broad, mushy everyone down to a specific, findable, motivated who.

You slice the vague group into narrower and narrower segments, asking within each one, who inside this group feels the pain most acutely, who has the money and the authority to solve it, and can I actually find and reach these people.

You keep slicing until you land on a group that is specific enough that you know where they hang out, what they read, what conferences they go to, motivated enough that they actually want the problem solved, and reachable enough that you can go talk to them this week.
That specific, findable, motivated who is worth more than a giant fuzzy everyone, because you can actually go learn from them and eventually sell to them.
Let me now walk you through what a bad conversation sounds like versus a good one, side by side, because the contrast makes it click.
The bad founder walks in and says, hi, I am building an app that helps people organize their recipes on their iPad, do you think that is a good idea, would you use something like that.
And the person, being kind, says, oh yeah, that sounds useful, I would probably use it.
And the founder writes down, strong interest, and leaves.
Everything about that exchange was poison.
He pitched immediately.
He asked for an opinion about his idea.
He asked a would question about a hypothetical future.
And he harvested a compliment and a piece of fluff and recorded it as data.
Now the good founder.
She sits down and says, so you do a lot of cooking, tell me about that.
And the person talks.
And she says, walk me through the last time you cooked something from a recipe.
Where did the recipe come from.
And the person says, oh, I just googled it, I never really save them, I tried this one app once but I stopped using it.
And she says, oh interesting, what made you stop.
And now she is learning that this person has already tried and abandoned a solution, which tells her the pain is real but the existing solutions failed, which is a whole map of the territory.
She never mentioned her idea.
She asked about the past.
She talked less and listened more.

And she walked out with facts, with quotes, with a real understanding of how this person actually lives around the problem, and crucially, she may have learned that her cookbook app is solving a problem people do not actually feel, which, if true, is the most valuable thing she could possibly learn, because it just saved her a year.
I want to spend a minute on that idea, because it is the emotional heart of the whole philosophy.
The goal of these conversations is not to hear yes.

The goal is to find the truth, and sometimes the truth is that your idea is bad, or that the problem you imagined is not painful enough, or that the people you thought were your customers do not actually care.

And a founder with the right mindset treats that discovery as a win, because you would rather learn it now, over coffee, in a week, than after you have quit your job and burned your savings and spent two years of your one wild and precious life building something nobody wanted.
The Mom Test is fundamentally a tool for failing fast and cheap on the ideas that deserve to fail, so that you can pour your real time into the ones that deserve to live.
It is not a technique for validating your idea.
It is a technique for stress testing your idea against reality, and hoping it survives, but being genuinely willing to hear that it did not.
Let me address a question that comes up constantly.
What about at some point, do you ever talk about your idea.
Yes.
Eventually.
Once you have genuinely understood the problem, once you have earned it, there is a place to introduce what you are building and to seek commitment.
But even then, the frame is not, do you like it.
The frame is, here is what I am building to solve the problem you just described, and then you ask for a real commitment.
Will you pre order.
Will you introduce me to your team.
Will you set up a pilot.
You are testing whether the interest is real by asking them to spend time, reputation, or money, not by asking them to spend compliments.
And if they will not commit anything, then all the nice words in the world do not matter, because they just told you the truth with their behavior, and behavior never lies the way words do.
There is also a piece of guidance about how to handle the fear of rejection and the discomfort of these conversations, because let us be honest, they are scary.
It is scary to ask someone about their life and risk hearing that your idea is pointless.
But Fitzpatrick reframes it.
You are not asking them to judge you.
You are asking them to teach you.
You are the student, they are the expert on their own life, and people love being the expert.

When you come in genuinely curious, genuinely humble, genuinely more interested in their world than in your idea, people open up, and the conversation stops being an audition and becomes a gift they are giving you.
The pressure evaporates.
You are not selling.
You are learning.
And that reframe, from salesperson to detective, from pitching to learning, from seeking approval to seeking truth, is the mental shift that makes all the mechanics actually work.
And while they talk, you take notes.
Real notes.
And here is a specific piece of craft that matters more than it seems.
Preserve their exact words.
Write down direct quotes, in their language, not your paraphrase, not your interpretation cleaned up into startup speak.
Because the exact quote carries information that your summary destroys.
If a customer says, I spend every Sunday night dreading the invoicing, that is a completely different signal than if you write down, customer has mild pain around billing.

The raw quote has the emotion, the frequency, the specific moment, the intensity, and later, when you are trying to decide what to build or trying to convince a cofounder or an investor that the pain is real, those exact words are your evidence.
So take good notes, preserve exact quotes, and capture not just what they said but the emotion behind it and whether they showed any real commitment.
Time, reputation, or money.
Let me also touch on the practical reality of keeping this going over time, because customer conversations are not a one time event you do at the start and then stop.
The best founders build a habit of it, a continuous, casual, low friction stream of learning from the people they serve.
It becomes part of how they work, not a phase.

And they do not delegate it entirely, especially early on, because the founder's own ear, hearing the exact words, feeling the intensity of the pain firsthand, is what shapes the intuition that guides every later decision.
You cannot fully outsource your understanding of your customer and expect to build something they love.
Now let me pull together the framework of how you actually use all of this to make the decision, because in the end that is the point.
You are trying to decide whether to build the thing, and if so, what exactly to build and for whom.
So after your conversations, you look at the real data, not the fluff.
You ask, is there a group of specific, findable people who have this problem badly.
Have they shown it is real by what they have already done and already spent to solve it.
When I described what I might build, did anyone commit real time, reputation, or money, or did I just get compliments.
And if the honest answer is that the pain is real, the people are findable, and some of them committed something that cost them, then you have signal worth acting on.

And if the honest answer is that everyone was nice but nobody had actually done anything about the problem and nobody would commit anything, then you have signal too, and the signal is stop, rethink, or walk away, and that is a victory, because you found out cheap.
So let me bring it all the way home with what you can actually do this week, because a book like this is worthless if it stays in your head and never touches your calendar.
Here is your assignment.
First, pick the scariest assumption underneath your idea, the one that, if it is wrong, the whole thing collapses.
Usually it is, does anyone actually have this problem badly enough to pay to solve it.
Write it down.
That is the center of your big three questions.
Second, write your big three.
The three things you most need to learn from a conversation, phrased as things you are afraid to find out.
Not soft questions.
The scary ones.
Third, find three real people this week who fit a specific, findable, motivated segment.
Not everyone.
A narrow who.
And go have casual conversations with them.
Coffee, a call, a hallway chat.
Keep it light.
Do not schedule a big formal thing if you can avoid it.
Fourth, and this is the hard part, do not pitch.
Keep your idea in your pocket.
Ask about their life.
Ask them to walk you through the last time they hit the problem.
Ask what they did, what it cost them, what they have already tried, and what they have already paid for.
Every time you catch yourself about to say, so I am building, bite your tongue and ask another question about their past instead.
Fifth, run the bad data radar the entire time.
When you hear a compliment, deflect it and dig.
When you hear fluff, an I always or an I would, anchor them to a specific moment in the past.
When you hear a feature idea, do not write it on a build list, dig for the motivation underneath it.
Steer, gently, always back to facts about their real life.
Sixth, before you leave every conversation, ask, so what happens next, and pay attention to whether they offer any real commitment.
Time, reputation, or money.
Write down whether this conversation actually advanced or just felt nice.
Seventh, take notes with exact quotes.
Their words, their emotion, not your cleaned up paraphrase.
You will thank yourself later when you are staring at the decision of what to build and you have their raw voice on the page.
And finally, be brave enough to hear that your idea might be bad, and treat that as the best possible outcome if it is true, because it means you just bought back years of your life.
Remember why the book is called what it is called.
You are learning to ask questions so good that even your own mother, who loves you more than anything and would never willingly hurt you, could not lie to you if she tried.
Because you never asked her whether your business is a good idea.
You asked her about her life, and you listened, and you let the truth come out on its own.
That is The Mom Test by Rob Fitzpatrick.
Talk about their life, not your idea.
Ask about specifics in the past, not opinions about the future.
Talk less and listen more.
Ignore compliments, cut through fluff, mine ideas for their underlying motivation.
Chase commitment in the currency of time, reputation, and money.
Prepare your big three, keep it casual, take good notes, and go find your specific who.
Do that, and you will stop building things nobody wants, and you will start building things people are quietly desperate for.
The person sitting across from you was never actually obligated to tell you the truth.
It was always your job to ask so well that they could not help but do it anyway.
So go have three real conversations this week.
Keep your idea in your pocket.
And listen.
The truth is out there, and now you know how to ask for it.
Go build something people actually need.
